title: How much does it cost to run an electric towel rail?
description: What an electric towel rail costs to run in the UK: real per-hour and per-year figures at the July 2026 price cap, and the timer habit that cuts the bill hard.
blurb: Per-hour and per-year electric towel rail costs at current UK rates, plus the timer habit that cuts them by 80 per cent.
lede: An electric towel rail is one of those quiet appliances that never asks for attention. It sits on the bathroom wall, keeps the towels warm, and because it never roars into life like a kettle or a fan heater, most people have no idea what it costs. The honest answer is that it depends almost entirely on two things: the wattage stamped on the element, and whether you run it on a timer or leave it on around the clock. The gap between those two habits is the difference between a rail that costs less than £25 a year and one that quietly adds £350 or more to your electricity bill.
short_answer: A typical 150W electric towel rail costs about 4p an hour to run, based on the July to September 2026 price cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. Left on 24 hours a day it costs roughly £343 a year, but on a timer for four hours a day the same rail costs about £57 a year. Bigger 450W to 600W rails can pass £1,000 a year if left running constantly, which is why a timer or thermostat matters more than the rail itself.

sections.h2: What an electric towel rail actually draws
sections.html:
Electric towel rails come in three broad types, and the wattage varies far more than most people expect:

  • Dry element ladder rails are the most common in UK bathrooms. A small cloakroom rail might be 60W to 80W, a standard family bathroom ladder is usually 100W to 150W, and a tall or wide one can reach 250W.
  • Oil or fluid filled rails behave more like a small electric radiator. They take longer to warm up, hold heat for longer, and typically run from 150W up to 600W. Some are sold with the idea that they heat the whole bathroom, not just the towels.
  • Dual fuel rails are plumbed into the central heating loop but also have an electric element, so you can run them off the boiler in winter and switch to electric-only in summer when the heating is off.

Towel rails do not carry the coloured efficiency rating you see on fridges and washing machines, because every watt they draw turns into heat. There is no efficient or inefficient model in the sense that energy labels measure; a 150W rail simply produces 150W of heat. The only number that matters is the one on the rating plate, usually on the element housing at the bottom of the rail, or in the manual.

That makes the maths simple. The same formula that works for any appliance applies here: wattage divided by 1,000 gives you kilowatts, multiply by hours used, then by your unit rate. A 150W rail running for 4 hours uses 0.6 kWh, which costs about 16p at the current cap.

sections.h2: Running costs per hour and per year
sections.html:
Here is what common towel rail sizes cost at the current price cap, comparing a sensible timer schedule of four hours a day against leaving the rail on permanently:

Rail wattageCost per hour4 hours a day, per yearOn 24/7, per year
60W (small cloakroom)1.6p£23£137
100W (standard ladder)2.6p£38£229
150W (large ladder)3.9p£57£343
300W (fluid filled)7.8p£114£686
450W (large fluid filled)11.7p£172£1,029
600W (rail as room heater)15.7p£229£1,372

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Annual figures assume the element draws full power whenever it is on; rails with a thermostat will cost somewhat less because they cycle off once up to temperature.

Two things jump out of that table. First, a small rail on a timer is genuinely cheap, at well under £1 a week. Second, the 24/7 column is brutal at every wattage. The rail itself is not the problem; the habit is.

Towel rails are one of the classic always-on loads, in the same family as the broadband router and the set-top box that never sleeps. The difference is scale. A router idles at around 10W; a towel rail draws 10 to 60 times that, every hour, all year. If you have ever wondered why a bathroom appliance you barely think about could matter, this is the same logic covered in our guide to standby and always-on power, just with a much bigger number attached.

The trap usually happens in one of three ways:

  • No switch in reach. Many rails are wired to a fused spur with the switch outside the bathroom or hidden behind a door, so turning it off never becomes a habit.
  • Summer creep. The rail goes on in October for good reasons and is still running in July because nobody thought about it. Six months of unnecessary running on a 150W rail is roughly £170 at current rates.
  • The damp towel argument. People leave the rail on because towels dry slowly otherwise. That is a real problem, but a timer solves it for a fraction of the cost, because towels do most of their drying in the first few hours.

If your rail has been on since last autumn, switching it off for the summer or putting it on a short timer is one of the easiest double-digit savings in the house.

sections.h2: Timers and thermostats: the cheap way to run one
sections.html:
The single biggest cost decision is not which rail you buy but how it is controlled. In rough order of cost effectiveness:

A timed schedule

Two hours in the morning around shower time and two hours in the evening covers warm towels when you actually touch them, and dries them after use. That four-hour pattern is what the £57 a year figure in the table assumes. If your rail is on a fused spur, an electrician can swap the switch for a boost timer or a programmable timer fuse spur for a modest cost. Plug-in rails can use an ordinary £10 plug timer.

A thermostatic element

Many modern rails have an element with a built-in thermostat that cycles the power once the rail reaches temperature. This typically cuts consumption noticeably compared with a basic on/off element, because the rail spends part of each hour drawing nothing. A thermostat plus a timer is the best of both.

A boost button

Some smart elements offer a one-touch boost that runs the rail for one or two hours and then shuts off. This suits households with irregular routines, because nothing is ever left on by accident.

One thing not to do: do not use a towel rail as the bathroom's main heater unless it is sized for it. A 150W ladder rail will take the chill off towels but will not meaningfully warm a cold room. If the bathroom itself is the problem, a properly sized radiator or a short blast from a different heat source is better value, and our guide to saving energy in the bathroom covers the wider picture.

sections.h2: Electric rail vs central heating rail
sections.html:
If your towel rail is plumbed into the central heating, the heat comes from gas at 7.33p per kWh rather than electricity at 26.11p. Allowing for a boiler running at around 85 per cent efficiency, heat from the gas loop costs roughly 8.6p per kWh delivered, about a third of the electric price. The catch is that a central heating rail is only warm when the heating runs, which usually means cold towels all summer.

That is exactly the problem dual fuel rails solve. In winter the rail runs off the boiler at gas prices; in summer you switch on the electric element only when you want it, ideally on a timer. For anyone renovating a bathroom with central heating already in the room, dual fuel is usually the cheapest arrangement to live with.

If you are choosing between an electric towel rail and a small electric radiator for a bathroom without central heating, the running cost per kWh is identical because both are simple resistance heaters. The difference is purpose: a rail warms towels well and rooms poorly, while a radiator does the opposite. Our electric radiator running cost guide covers the room-heating side of that decision.

One more option for off-peak households: if you are on Economy 7 or another time-of-use tariff, a fluid-filled rail timed to heat on cheap-rate electricity overnight will hold some warmth into the morning, though it will not stay warm all day the way a storage heater does.

An electric towel rail is cheap or expensive depending almost entirely on how long it runs. The numbers worth remembering:

  • A typical 100W to 150W ladder rail costs 2.6p to 3.9p an hour.
  • On a four-hour daily timer, expect £38 to £57 a year. That is reasonable money for dry, warm towels every day.
  • Left on 24/7, the same rail costs £229 to £343 a year, and a big fluid-filled rail can pass £1,000.
  • A timer, a thermostatic element, or simply flicking the switch off after the morning shower captures most of the saving with no loss of comfort.
  • If the rail can run off the boiler, use gas in winter and the element only in summer.

Check the wattage plate on your own rail, multiply by the hours it actually runs, and you will know your real cost in under a minute. For most households the honest verdict is that the rail was never the villain; the missing timer was.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to leave an electric towel rail on all the time?
faq.a: No. A towel rail converts every watt to heat whether you need it or not, so leaving it on simply multiplies the cost. A 150W rail costs about £343 a year running 24/7 at the July to September 2026 cap rate, against about £57 on a four-hour daily timer. Towels dry within a few hours, so the extra 20 hours a day buys almost nothing.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a towel rail overnight?
faq.a: A 150W rail running for 8 hours uses 1.2 kWh, which costs about 31p at 26.11p per kWh. Do that every night and it adds up to around £114 a year, which is why overnight running only makes sense on a cheap off-peak tariff, and even then only with a fluid-filled rail that holds its heat into the morning.

faq.q: Will an electric towel rail heat my bathroom?
faq.a: Only partially. A standard 100W to 150W ladder rail is designed to warm towels, not air, and will barely change the room temperature in a typical bathroom. Fluid-filled rails of 450W to 600W can act as the main heat source in a small bathroom, but at those wattages a timer or thermostat is essential to keep costs sensible.

faq.q: Are dual fuel towel rails worth it?
faq.a: Usually yes, if the bathroom already has central heating pipework. Heat from a gas boiler costs roughly a third of the electric equivalent per kWh, so running the rail off the heating loop in winter and using the electric element only in summer gives you warm towels year round at the lowest running cost.

faq.q: Does the size or brand of towel rail change the running cost?
faq.a: Only through wattage. There is no efficiency difference between brands because all the electricity becomes heat. A 150W rail from any manufacturer costs the same to run. Pick the rail on size, build quality and whether the element has a thermostat and timer, since the controls are what actually determine the bill.
